mappings.rationaleAI: The Mamluks' defeat of the Mongols at Ain Jalut (1260), their expulsion of the Crusaders from the Levant, and the Bahri/Burji dynasties directly map to B.4.b.ii (Mamluk resistance to Mongols under Baybars) and the broader pattern of Muslim response to crusader and Mongol invasions.

The Almoravid Empire's origins as a religious reform federation, conquest of Morocco, intervention in Spain against Christian states, and eventual displacement by the Almohads directly correspond to B.4.c.i on the Almoravid conquests and decline.

Byzantium's relations with Crusaders, the Great Schism of 1054, and the empire's fall in 1453 directly map to A.1.c (Byzantine Empire 1081-1204), A.1.c.iii (Fourth Crusade and Latin Empire), and A.3 (Palaeologus restoration).

The Third Crusade (1189-1192) with Richard I, Philip II, and Frederick Barbarossa, the Siege of Acre, and the negotiated truce with Saladin directly correspond to A.1.b on the Second and Third Crusades.

Melisende, Queen of Jerusalem (1105-1161), her co-rule with Fulk of Anjou and regency for Baldwin III, directly illustrate the governance of the Crusader Kingdom relevant to A.1.b on crusader states and their institutions.
The siege and fall of Constantinople in 1453 by Mehmed II directly corresponds to A.3.c (final Turkish assault and fall of Constantinople) and B.3.b.i (Ottoman conquest of Constantinople).

The Great Schism of 1054 and the deepening estrangement through the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople (1204) directly correspond to A.1.c on Byzantine relations with the crusaders and the Fourth Crusade.

Muslim Spain from the taifa kingdoms through Almoravid and Almohad rule and the Reconquista directly corresponds to B.4.c (Almoravid and Almohad empires in Spain) and B.4.d (late medieval dynasties including the Nasrid kingdom of Granada).

The Knights Templar's military role in Crusader states, their banking innovations, and dramatic suppression by Philip IV in 1307-1312 correspond to A.1.b (crusader states and their institutions) and A.1.d.ii (later crusades and survival of crusading spirit).

Venice's territorial acquisitions after the Fourth Crusade (1204), rivalry with Genoa, and commercial inroads into the Levantine trade directly correspond to A.1.c.iii (Fourth Crusade) and A.1.d on later crusading commercial expansion.
The Volga Vikings' foundation of the Kievan Rus and trade with the Abbasid Caliphate provide essential background for A.1.e on Russia (1054-1300) and the lands of Rus before the Mongol invasion.

The Prester John legend's origins in 12th-century European chronicles and its connection to the Crusades and hopes for Christian allies against Islam directly relate to the crusading movement's ideological context (A.1.a.i, A.1.d).

The Baltic Crusades provide comparison and contrast with the Holy Land campaigns as part of the broader crusading movement, relevant to A.1 on the crusading era, though the Baltic focus is more peripheral.
Bohemia's Hussite Wars as a proto-Reformation movement are relevant to the broader late medieval context, though the section's focus is primarily on eastern Christendom and the crusading movement.
